coredata.dc:title: Aspects of luminescence nanoprobes for thermometry: Progress and outlook
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Pawan
Patel, Rajkumar
Shrivastava, Navadeep
Patel, Madhumita
Rondeau-Gagné, Simon
Selopal, Gurpreet Singh
coredata.dc:description: The measurement of correct temperature with precise accuracy is crucial to conduct both scientific research and industrial manufacturing. A conventional thermometer is based on the thermal expansion of materials and Seebeck effect. Therefore, conventional thermometers are not considered to be accurate for measuring the temperature at submicrometric spatial resolution. An urge for an innovative non-contact thermometer sensor encourages the development of luminescence thermometry as an attractive alternative for sensing and monitoring the temperature with high accuracy and preciseness. In this context, luminescence thermometry has emerged as an attractive field of research due to the exceptional properties of luminescent materials and its use in various applications of as-designed luminescence thermometry in chemistry, biomedicine, or electromagnetism. The present review summarizes the recent advances in developing, designing, and constructing luminescence thermometry using novel luminescent materials. The significance of luminescence thermotropy is discussed and described. Various effects of temperature on photoluminescence (PL) features and their detection modes are discussed. The review covers the different luminescent materials used for temperature sensing or thermometry; lanthanides doped materials, quantum dots, core@shell nanomaterials. organic fluorophores, hybrid nanomaterials. Conclusively, this review provides new direction to identify appropriate luminescent materials for luminescence thermometry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Thermometry
Lanthanide
Nanomaterials
Quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The measurement of correct temperature with precise accuracy is crucial to conduct both scientific research and industrial manufacturing. A conventional thermometer is based on the thermal expansion of materials and Seebeck effect. Therefore, conventional thermometers are not considered to be accurate for measuring the temperature at submicrometric spatial resolution. An urge for an innovative non-contact thermometer sensor encourages the development of luminescence thermometry as an attractive alternative for sensing and monitoring the temperature with high accuracy and preciseness. In this context, luminescence thermometry has emerged as an attractive field of research due to the exceptional properties of luminescent materials and its use in various applications of as-designed luminescence thermometry in chemistry, biomedicine, or electromagnetism. The present review summarizes the recent advances in developing, designing, and constructing luminescence thermometry using novel luminescent materials. The significance of luminescence thermotropy is discussed and described. Various effects of temperature on photoluminescence (PL) features and their detection modes are discussed. The review covers the different luminescent materials used for temperature sensing or thermometry; lanthanides doped materials, quantum dots, core@shell nanomaterials. organic fluorophores, hybrid nanomaterials. Conclusively, this review provides new direction to identify appropriate luminescent materials for luminescence thermometry.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature is one of the fundamental parameters used to measure physical properties such as specific/latent heat, thermal expansion coefficient, thermal conductivity, elastic or Young's modulus, yield strength, diffusion and stress of flow, stiffness, etc. in scientific and industrial communities [1–4]. Consequently, temperature sensors used in various applications in daily life in various fields such as metrology, aerodynamics, climate research, medicine, military technology, bio-medical, air conditioning, food storage, and other goods, mining research etc. [1,3,5-12] According to an estimation, 75–80% of the world's market sensors are temperature sensors with an estimated ∼ $6.86 billion by 2023 [3,5,13]. Owing to use in multiple applications of temperature sensing, different types of thermometers are required depending upon the different environments of uses [1,14]. Therefore, various thermometry techniques have been developed depending on the fundamental and practical application [1]. Generally, temperature sensing thermometry can be divided into three types; (1) liquid-filled glass thermometers based on the thermal expansion of materials, (2) thermocouples based on the Seebeck effect, and (3) optical sensors [3].

Among these three-temperature sensing thermometry, optical sensor-based temperature sensors have attracted great interest due to many advantages, including non-contact measurement, electrical connection free, insensitive to magnetic fields, large-scale imaging, infrared thermometers, and application in various areas like biomedical field, environmental observations, industries [15–18]. Moreover, an optical sensor has many other benefits, such as smaller in size, tiny thermal mass, and quick thermal detection. Additionally, optical sensors are not limited to temperature measurement only. The optical sensor can be compensated during optical sensing of analytes such as pH, O2, or CO2. For example, the temperature is essential in optical sensing of O2 because luminescence quenching by oxygen is highly temperature dependent [15,19-21].

Several optical sensor-based thermometers have been developed based on various temperature-depended optical properties such as absorption, thermal reflection, Rayleigh and Raman scattering, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) thermometry, and luminescence [3,15,22-24]. Among them, luminescent materials-based thermometry is a promising approach owing to its numerous benefits like high resolution and precision, quick response, and non-invasive [1,25]. Luminescent materials used is used for temperature sensing by implementing other optical fiber sensing arrangements; the fiber itself acts as sensing element where luminescent material is placed on tip of fiber, and remote sensing where luminescent material is not directly attached to the fiber. In a remote sensing scheme, the luminescent material is either embedded in an optode or optrode (an optical sensor device) or painted on the surface where temperature measurement is required [15,26,27]. Generally, most luminescent materials are predicted to exhibit temperature depending on luminescence features; emission peak energy and intensity, luminescence lifetime and band shape etc. However, luminescent materials that show the strong effect of temperature on luminescence properties are considered promising candidates for practical applications. This last sentence is not connecting.

In past years, numerous luminescent materials such as lanthanide doped, quantum dots, organic compounds, nanoparticles, core-shell nanocrystals, and hybrid nanomaterials have been developed and proposed for temperature sensing, which depends upon sensitivity, temperature range, and stability. This article highlights the progress and development of various luminescent materials for thermometry application applications and summarizes these research endeavors. Additionally, we attempt to make a general survey of the most recent progress in luminescence-based thermometry. First, the dependence of luminescence features will be discussed, emission intensity, the shape of the emission band, decay time, and effect of polarization on temperature. Then, we will focus on developing various luminescent materials; lanthanide-based materials, nanomaterials, quantum dots, core-shell and hybrid materials, and organic compounds as thermometry probes. Finally, the challenges and perspectives of the present optical thermometric based on luminescent materials will be presented.

Luminescence is defined as the emission of light from a substance when the electronic transitions of electrons from excited states to ground states occurs [3,14,28]. The excited states are populated by an external excitation source. Therefore, luminescence features strongly depend upon the properties and population of electronic states participating in photons emission. The electronic state involved in photon emission depends upon temperature. Consequently, the relation between the luminescence properties and local temperature is used for thermal sensing of a substance termed luminescence thermometry. The emission spectrum of a luminescent material is including various parameters; emission peak intensity, shape of emission peak, position of peak maxima, polarization, lifetime/decay-time, and width of emission band/peak. The luminescence thermometry exploits how one or more parameters vary with temperature [5,14].

Luminescent thermometry is a non-contact, excellent accuracy, and sensitivity technique for temperature measurement. Almost every parameter such as emission peak intensity, the shape of emission peak, the position of peak maxima, polarization, lifetime/decay-time, and width of emission band/peak related to fluorescence emission can serve for measuring temperature sensing. Schematic 1 shows variation in each of these parameters with temperature which can be used for temperature measurement (thermometry measurement). Therefore, several methods have been developed for temperature sensing or thermometry using luminescent materials. Consequently, the luminescence thermometry methods can be classified into various types depending on the temperature change in luminescent properties. Those different type of luminescence thermometry methods is defined as follows depending upon the parameter used for monitoring temperature: [5,14,29-32]
(i)
Thermometry based on fluorescence emission intensity: The temperature change alters the number of emitting photons per second, leading to variation in luminescent intensity. Therefore, luminescent thermometry (or thermal sensing) is determined by analysis of change in fluorescence emission intensity of luminescent material.
(ii)
Thermometry based on emission band-shape: The change in the band-shape of emission spectrum of luminescent material occurs when electronic states attributing to emission are close in energies and thermally coupled. The change in the band shape of the emission spectrum can also be present in a mixed system with more than one emitting center. In this method, change in the shape of the emission spectrum is exploited for thermometry measurement.
(iii)
Thermometry based on spectral position: By analyzing the difference in the emission spectrum's spectral position caused by the energy levels of the two electronic states engaged in the emission, luminescent thermometry (or thermal sensing) is determined. This depends on a wide range of temperature-dependent temperature factors, such as the refractive index and interatomic distances, of the emitting material.
(iv)
Thermometry based on bandwidth of emission spectrum: The analysis of change in bandwidth of emission spectrum of luminescent material used to evaluate the luminescent thermometry (or thermal sensing). Because the density of phonons enhanced with rise in the temperature which result in broadening of the emission spectrum. In general, near-room temperature homogeneous line broadening results in a linear connection between bandwidth and temperature.
(v)
Thermometry based on polarization luminescence: The luminescent thermometry (or thermal sensing) of luminescent material is influenced by analysis of change in shape and intensity of emission spectrum under its polarization. In an anisotropic media, the emitted radiation from luminescent material is generally non-isotropically polarized, consequently, the shape and intensity of emitted radiation strongly depend on its polarization.
(vi)
Thermometry based on lifetime or decay time: The lifetime of luminescent materials depends upon the number of factors, such as assisted ET (energy transfer) processes and multi-phonon decays, which depend upon temperature. Due to the temperature dependence of lifetime, it (temperature) can be easily extracted from the calculation of the luminescence lifetime and utilized for thermometry measurement.

The luminescent materials or phosphors have gained broad interest from researchers because of their excellent optical properties which play significant roles in numerous applications such as optoelectronic devices, light emitting diodes (LEDs), solid-state laser, biomedical, anti-counterfeiting, sensing, luminescent solar concentration etc. [28,33-44] Because luminescence is sensitive to temperature change, therefore the use of luminescent materials and their composites as probes for thermometry has emerged as a promising field of research in the last few decades [45]. As discussed above change in luminescence characteristic of any material can be monitored by observing changes in different parameters such as lifetime, emission intensity, spectral shift, spectral width, polarization anisotropy and emission intensity ratios of two peaks. Consequently, these different parameters have been exploited for thermometry in recent times. In this section, we aim to provide in-depth insight into use of various materials such lanthanides doped materials, quantum dots, core@shell materials. organic fluorophores, hybrid nanomaterials for temperature sensing or thermometry.

The lanthanide-doped materials have undergone intensive research due to their potential for use in a variety of applications [46–51]. Among others lanthanide doped luminescent materials have emerged as temperature sensing [3,52,53]. Various lanthanide ions such as Eu3+, Tb3+, Tm3+, Er3+, Tb3+ etc., are studied in temperature sensing due to narrow emission (f-f transitions) and their strongly dependent on temperature [54,55]. Several studies have been reported on temperature sensing of lanthanide-doped luminescence micro/nanomaterials. En-Hai Song et al. reported the change in luminescence intensity in yellow upconversion in KZnF3:Yb3+, Mn2+ nanocrystals [56]. The upconversion KZnF3:Yb3+, Mn2+ nanocrystals show broad yellow emission at 585 nm when excited with a 976 nm laser diode which is recognized as the 4T1g(G) to 6A1g (S) transition of Mn2+ion. The KZnF3:Yb3+, Mn2+ nanocrystals exhibit the temperature depending on luminescent intensity during cooling and heating. The yellow emission intensity decreases during heating from 300 K to 573 K and increases during cooling, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a, b). Xu et al. studied the impact of temperature on upconversion (FIR) fluorescence intensity ratio between 700 (Tm3+; 3F2,3 → 3H6) and 800 nm (Tm3+; 3H4→3H6) in Tm3+/Yb3+ co-doped oxyfluoride glass ceramic upon excitation wavelength of 980 nm [57]. The Tm3+/Yb3+ co-doped oxyfluoride glass ceramic show upconversion FIR between 700 nm and 800 nm, increased with increase in temperature from 293703 K. This study demonstrated that the obtained thermometry sensitivity is high and consistent, as shown in Fig. 1(c). In another study, the impact of the local matrix neighborhood, the ligand type and the symmetry on thermal sensitivity of Er3+was discussed [58]. The thermal sensitivity of upconversion luminescence in Er3+ was analyzed along with the ligands type and bond character within the framework of the Judd–Ofelt theory. This study demonstrates that the best-thermalized emissions to use as optical temperature sensors are those in which the high energy emitting level is hypersensitive to the local matrix neighborhood as the more disordered the local neighborhood of the active ion, the better the FIR of emission peaks and thermal sensitivity [58]. The upconversion emission intensity ratio between the green and the near-infrared emissions in two host lattices; an oxyfluoride glass and a nanocrystalline fluoride-type glass-ceramic from room temperature (RT) to 720 K was compared. Sedlmeier and co-workers d the ratiometric temperature response of core-only and core-shell structures of hexagonal NaYF4 host lattice doped with various combinations [59] The effect of molybdenum doping on temperature sensing sensitivity on the Tm-Yb and Er-Yb doped different host matrices such as TiO2, Al2O3, Yb3Al5O12 (YbAG), and Gd2O3 [60]. The obtained result shows that the intensity ratio between green/blue upconversion emission in the Er(Tm)-Yb-Mo doped host matrix is about four orders in magnitude more efficient than in the Er(Tm)-Yb doped host matrix without Mo co-doping.

Further, Zhou et al. demonstrated the temperature sensing ability of hydrothermally synthesized pure β-NaYF4:20%Yb3+, 2%Er3+ micro prisms [64]. The temperature dependence upconversion emission intensity ratio of emission bands at 525 nm and 545 nm at excitation of 980 nm from 160 K to 300Kwas studied. The relative theoretical sensitivity of intensity ratio is 1.20% K − 1 at 300 K. In this report, it was demonstrated that relative deviation of 0.5% in the upconversion fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) of emission bands at 525 nm and 545 nm is measurable by a conventional spectrometer and a variation of 0.4⁰C. The impact of Er3+ concentration on the optical temperature sensitivity of fluorotellurite glass was investigated by Sergio et al. [61] In this study, researchers determined that the temperature sensitivity of upconversion luminescence-based temperature sensors highly depended on radiative energy transfer processes, which can be avoided by using a comparatively low concentration of Er3+ions. Therefore, the concentration of Er3+ion is an essential factor in developing an Er3+ based upconversion optical temperature sensor. The increase in concentration of Er3+ions, decreases the ratio of the intensity of emission bands of Er3+ions; therefore, the sensitivity of the thermal sensor also decreases. This issue can be resolved by using low conc. of Er3+ions. The relation between thermal sensitivity and concentration of Er3+ions is illustrated in Fig. 1(d). Wang et al. investigated the application of core-shell upconversion NaYbF4:Tm3+@SiO2 microparticles as the temperature sensor [65]. The optical thermometry of NaYbF4:Tm3+@SiO2 microparticles was tested in the temperature range 100–700 K using upconversion FIR of emission bands corresponding to 1D2/1G4 and 3F2 /3H4 levels.

The tetragonal Dy3+ doped BaYF5 nanoparticles for the temperature sensor were reported by Cao et al. [66] The behavior of fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) increase with temperature in the range of 550 - 800 K was investigated. The FIR shows the linear increment with the increase in temperature from 550 - 800 K, which reveals the potential use of Dy3+ doped BaYF5 nanoparticles for temperature sensing applications. Savchuk et al. reported the Er:Yb:NaYF4 and Er:Yb:NaY2F5O upconversion nanocrystals (NCs) as temperature sensor based on lifetime of emission from these nanophosphors [67]. The temperature sensitivities of Er:Yb:NaYF4 and Er:Yb:NaY2F5O phosphors was compared and Er:Yb:NaY2F5O NCs show better thermal sensitivity than Er:Yb:NaYF4. Moreover, this study also demonstrated the use of these thermal sensor for ex-vivo experiments by injecting the Er:Yb:NaY2F5O up-converting nanoparticles. Lojpur et al. reported Y2O3:Yb3+, Er3+ upconversion phosphor for temperature sensor below room temperature in the range of 10–300 K [62]. In this study, the temperature depends of the FIR between upconversion emission from 2H11/2 and 4S3/2 levels of Er3+ was examined. The reliance of the FIR in the temperature range 10 K to 300 K is illustrated in Fig. 1(e). The relationship between temperature and the FIR in Er3+-Yb3+ doped TeO2–WO3 upconversion phosphor was reported by Pandey et al. [63] The variation in fluorescent intensity ratio in Er3+-Yb3+ doped TeO2–WO3 upconversion with temperature shown in Fig. 1(f). The Er3+-Yb3+ doped TeO2–WO3 upconversion shows a temperature sensitivity of 28.72 × 10−4 K − 1 up to 690 K.

Recently, multifunctional or multi-mode lanthanide-doped materials have been explored for temperature sensing. Chai et al. demonstrated the dual-mode Er3+doped Ba0.4Ca0.6TiO3 ferroelectric phosphor for temperature sensor in the range of 103–573 K at excitation 980 nm [68]. The variation in FIR is illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). In another study, the authors investigate variation in the thermometry sensitivity with the crystallinity of the host lattice [69]. The effect of crystallinity on temperature sensitivity of Tm3+ doped LiNbO3 (host lattice) at excitation 980 nm was examined. The upconversion emission from 3F2,3 and 3H4 energy levels of Tm3+ ion was thermally coupled levels in both polycrystal and single-crystal phases. In this study, it was observed that there was a substantial impact of crystallinity of host lattice on the thermometry sensitivity of Tm3+ doped LiNbO3 upconversion phosphor. The change in crystallinity of the host lattice leads to a change in upconversion emission and a fractional change in upconversion had a significant change in temperature sensitivity. The effect of temperature and crystallinity of the host lattice on thermometry sensitivity is shown in Fig. 2(b). The Figure shows that the polycrystal host lattice has higher temperature sensitivity than single crystal, which further has increases with the sintering temperature increase. The temperature sensitivity of lanthanum oxyfluoride (LaOF) doped with Eu3+ phosphor was explored by Rakov and co-workers [70]. The fluorescence quenching in LaOF:Eu3+ was investigated with temperature. The non-radiative energy transfer between the (Eu3+)5D2 state and CTS (charge transfer states) of the LaOF host lattice was evaluated by fluorescence dynamics of LaOF:Eu3+ as a function of temperature. The effect of temperature on fluorescence decay time constant (dynamics of fluorescence of LaOF:Eu3+) is illustrated in Fig. 2(c). Venturini et al. synthesized Cr3+:YAB phosphor and examined this phosphor's optical thermometry sensing properties [15]. The fluorescence emission of Cr3+:YAB phosphor strongly deepened on temperature, revealing that this phosphor could be a potential contender for the temperature sensor. This study investigated the effect of temperature on luminescence emission decay in a broad temperature range. The change in the luminescence emission decay profile of the Cr3+:YAB phosphor is shown in Fig. 2(d). The Fig. 2(d) shows the decrease in decay time with increase in temperature. Sedmak and co-workers performed transient submicron temperature imaging based on upconversion Er/Yb co-doped glass ceramic [71].

The change in the emission spectrum and its intensity was examined in the temperature range of 25⁰C to 150⁰C. The author reported that transient thermal imaging across 200 µm × 200 µm field of view has a spatial resolution of ∼400 nm at an imaging frequency of 7 Hz. The calculated temperature accuracy was within ±10 K. The exceptional optical, mechanical and photo-thermal stability of a synthesized, transparent Er/Yb co-doped glass ceramic suggested that Er/Yb co-doped glass ceramic could be a promising candidate for the transient thermal imaging of hot spots and thermal gradients. The distribution and rate of change of the temperature with time for the analyzed hot-spot temperature field during the heating cycle are shown in Fig. 3 (a). The inset of Fig. 3(a) shows the 2D plot of the transient temperature field. The multimode temperature sensing performance of Sm2+-doped SrB4O7 phosphor was investigated by Cao et al. [72] The FIR and fluorescence lifetime dependence were examined in a wide temperature range of 300 to 723 K. The FIR of Sm2+-doped SrB4O7 phosphor increases with temperature, while fluorescence lifetime decreases with temperature, as shown in Figs. 3(b) and (c). The emission color of Sm2+-doped SrB4O7 phosphor changes from red to green with increase in temperature (300 to 723 K) which was visible to the human eye (Fig. 3(d)). The FIR and fluorescence lifetime thermometry sensitivity were 2.16%K − 1 and 3.36% K − 1, respectively. In another study, Mahata et al. investigated the dependent energy migration in YVO4: Ho3+/Yb3+ phosphor for low-temperature sensing application [73]. It was observed that the time of energy migration from host lattice (YVO4) to lanthanide ion (Ho3+) increased from 0.157 μs to 0.514 μs as temperature decreased from 300 K to 12 K. Fig. 3(e) show the change in FIR and temperature sensitivity with temperature for YVO4: Ho3+/Yb3+ phosphor. The temperature sensing performance of dual-functional β-NaYF4: Yb3+, Er3+ nanoparticles was explored by Hua et al. [74] The dependency of FIR of 521 nm and 541 nm emissions bands (upon excitation of 980 nm) on temperature was observed in this report. The change in the FIR of 521 nm and 541 nm emissions bands is shown in Fig. 3(f). Fig. 3(f) reveals that FIR increases with the increase in temperature due to variation in the thermal population. In another report, Tian et al. investigated the effect of co-dopant (Yb3+) on temperature sensitivity of Yb3+/Er3+ co-doped YNbO4 nanoparticles [75]. The author revealed that Yb3+/Er3+ co-doped YNbO4 nanoparticles (NPs) temperature sensitivity decreased with an increase in Yb3+ concentration (as shown in Fig. 4 (a)). The temperature sensitivity was reduced with the increase of Yb3+ions concentration because, at high concentrations, Yb3+ ions act as trapping centers due to back energy transfer from Er3+ to Yb3+. However, the concentration of Er3+ ions is also an important factor for optimizing upconversion phosphor for temperature sensing. The effect of Er3+ ions on the thermometry performance of NaGdTiO4: Er3+/Yb3+ phosphor was investigated by Li and co-workers [76]. The change in temperature sensitivity with 0.5, 5 and 20 mole% of Er3+ ions concentration in NaGdTiO4: Er3+/Yb3+ phosphor is shown in Fig. 4(b). The Figure (4b) showss that the thermometry sensitivity increases with; that the thermometry sensitivity increases with Er3+ ions concentration due to the different radiative transition rates of respectively excited state levels with 0.5, 5, and 20 mol % of ions Er3+ ions concentration. Further, the dependence of optical temperature sensing properties on Ho3+ doping in Er3+–Yb3+ doped La2CaZnO5 (LCZ) phosphor was explored by Kumar et al. [77] The effect of Ho3+ doping in Er3+–Yb3+ doped La2CaZnO5 phosphor on temperature sensor sensitivity in the temperature range 300–573 K is shown in Fig. 4(c). Fig. 4(c) reveals that the sensitivity increases with the concentration of Ho3+ ions from 1 to 9 mol%. Additionally, a comparison between the resolution of FIR temperature sensing of Er3+–Yb3+ doped La2CaZnO5 (LCZ) phosphor with or without doping of Ho3+ ions is demonstrated in Fig. 4(d).

Moreover, the excitation power is also very significant to decide the high-performance thermometry sensor with less error because the photons population might differ at different excitation pumping power. Wang and his co-worker reported the optical temperature sensing of Er3+ doped transparent Sr0.69La0.31F2.31 glass ceramics on excitation powder [78]. In this report, the effect of pump power on fluorescence intensity ratio, emission spectrum, population stability, temperature sensitivity and thermal quenching ratio were analyzed. It was observed that the pump power strongly depended on the fluorescence intensity ratio, emission spectrum, population stability, temperature sensitivity and thermal quenching ratio. The dependence of the emission spectrum of Er3+ doped Sr0.69La0.31F2.31 on temperature (298 K to 573 K) upon excitation wavelength 980 nm with two 66.8 mW/mm2 and 374.8 mW/mm2 is shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). It is observed that the intensity of green and red emission bands decreases with an increase in temperature while the intensity of infrared (IR) emission bands increases with temperature with a peak shift. The variation in optical temperature sensitivity for three thermally coupled energy levels (TCL)1 (2H11/2/4S3/2), TCL2 (4F9/2(1)/4F9/2(2)), and TCL3 (4I9/2(1)/4I9/2(2)) with excitation powder is shown in Fig. 5(c). Crystallinity has a significant impact on the thermometry sensing properties of lanthanide doped phosphor. Lu et al. reported the influence of incorporating Mn2+ ions in NaLuF4:Yb3+/Er3+ phosphor on temperature sensing properties [79]. They noticed that the temperature sensing properties of NaLuF4:Yb3+/Er3+ phosphor was improved with the incorporation of Mn2+ ion due to closed energy transfer (ET) between the Er3+ and Mn2+ [4S3/2 (Er3+) / 4T1 (Mn2+) / 4F9/2 (Er3+)]. The temperature dependence of FIR of NaLuF4:Yb3+/Er3+ phosphor with and without incorporation of Mn2+ ions is shown in Fig. 5(d). The enhancement in temperature sensing with the incorporation of Mn2+ ions in NaLuF4:Yb3+/Er3+ phosphor is higher in non-thermally coupled levels (non-TCLs) (as shown in Fig. 5(d)). This is due to fast ET between the Er3+ and Mn2+. The effect of K + ions on the down conversion phosphor was investigated by Singh et al. [80] The temperature dependence of photoluminescence emission with and without doping of K + ions in BaTiO3:Eu3+ was studied. The doping of K + ions in BaTiO3:Eu3+ results in a substantial increase in emission intensity. Xing et al. demonstrated multi-ratio optical thermometry based on TCLs and nonTCLs using different emission bands of double emission centers in BaNb2O6:Yb3+ /Er3+ /Tm3+phosphor with a sensitivity of 2.30% and in the range from 298 to 673 K [81]. In another study, Yunfei Qu et al. reported a comparative study of TCLs and non-TCLs based fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) thermometry in Ho3+/Yb3+/Tm3+ co-doped 12CaO·7Al2O3 (C12A7) [82]. Their study observed that non-TCLs show improved thermometry sensitivity because it is not limited by Boltzmann distribution. The FIR thermometry based on NTCLs shows higher sensitivity in the range of 363–523 K. The maximum values for relative and absolute sensitivities (SR and SA )were2.82 %K − 1 and 0.0207K−1, respectively which are higher than previously reports on TCLs thermometry of Ho3+ and Tm3+. Hang Liu and co-workers investigated Ho3+(Er3+)/Yb3+ doped SrWO4 phosphors for FIR thermometry based on TCLs and non-TCLs [83]. The maximum absolute sensitivity value 0.0158 K − 1 was achieved at 303 K with non-TCLs while 0.013 K − 1 at 393 K with TCLs thermometry. There are many other recent reports in which TCL and non-TCL thermometry were presented [84–89]. In recent time band single band approach of luminescence thermometry has become interesting for researchers where the intensity ratio of a single emission band is used to monitor the temperature. Trejgis et al. investigated single band ratio-metric luminescent thermometry for NaYF4:2%Nd3+ nanophosphor [90]. The two excitation wavelengths; 793 nm (the ground state) and1060 nm (for excited state) are used for analyze single band ratio-metric luminescence thermometry. The intensity of the band at 880 nm is used to monitor temperature at 793 nm and 1060 nm excitations wavelengths a temperature range of 273 to 473 K. Wang et al. single band radiometric thermometric based on the dependence of luminescence thermal quenching behavior on the excitation wavelength in a Pr3+:Gd2ZnTiO6 phosphor [91]. The intensity ratio emission peak was monitor at two different excitation wavelength 4521 nm and 340 nm in the temperature range of 298 K – 573 K. The Pr3+:Gd2ZnTiO6 phosphor shows sensing sensitivity, relative sensitivity and thermal uncertainty values at 1.74 K − 1, 3.64% K − 1 and 0.14 K), respectively. Additionally, single band ratiometric thermometry in similar phosphor such as Pr3+:Gd2MgTiO6, Pr3+:LiLaMgWO6 and Pr3+:La2ZnTiO6 were also explored. Similar other reports also demonstrated single band emission for thermometry [92–97]. Zheng et al. reported the luminescence thermometry based on dependence of bandwidth and lifetime on temperature in Mn4+ doped Gd2ZnTiO6 double perovskite phosphors [98]. The luminescence thermometry shows maximum relative sensitivity for Mn4+ doped Gd2ZnTiO6 double perovskite phosphors values at 0.34% K − 1 and 2.43% K − 1 for bandwidth and lifetime respectively. Lu et al. reported low temperature luminescence thermometry in YOF:Yb3+/Tm3+ phosphor by utilizing bandwidth of emission spectrum as sensing parameter in the range of 12–300 K [99]. The FWHMs of 12,485 cm−1(800.9 nm) and 12,580 cm−1(794.9 nm) emission bands are 14 cm−1(0.9 nm) and 12 cm−1(0.75 nm) at 12 K, respectively, which show exponential increase with increase in temperature from 12 K to 300 K. The maximum values of the absolute and relative sensitivities are 0.49 cm−1K−1(300 K) and 0.84% K − 1(300 K) for 12,485 cm−1(800.9 nm) emission bands and 0.38 cm−1K−1(300 K) and 0.67% K − 1(300 K) for 12,485 cm−1 (800.9 nm) and 12,580 cm−1 (794.9 nm) emission bands.

Cao et al. explored the temperature sensing performance of self-crystallized K3YF6:Er3+ phosphor [100]. The FIR of emission peaks at 525 and 545 nm increases with temperature in the range of 300 to 510 K at excited 980 nm as shown in Fig. 6 (a). This increment is due to enhancement in crystallinity of K3YF6:Er3+ phosphor with temperature. The dependence of temperature sensing on excitation wavelength and particle size of lanthanide NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ upconversion phosphor was reported by Liu et al. [101] This incorporates the temperature sensing propertie of particle sizes (21.6 nm and 64.3 nm) and excitation wavelengths (980 nm and 1550 nm). It was observed that there is no major effect on particles size and excitation wavelength properties. Although, the shorter wavelength and higher laser excitation density produce higher sample temperature. Additionally, the heating effect was more in the case of the larger particle as compared to small particles at the similar excitation wavelength and excitation density. Lu et al. reported the optical thermometry sensing properties of the orthorhombic phase Gd2(MoO4)3:Er3+/Yb3+film [102]. The upconversionemission spectra of Gd2(MoO4)3:Er3+/Yb3+film under excitation 980 nm is illustrated in Fig. 6(b). The inset shows an optical image of the green emission from Gd2(MoO4)3:Er3+/Yb3+film under excitation 980 nm. It was found that FIR of Gd2(MoO4)3:Er3+/Yb3+film was changed from 0.634 to 5.705 with the increase in temperature from 295 to 660 K. Additionally, high accuracy was achieved in Gd2(MoO4)3:Er3+/Yb3+film due to slight photo-thermal effect. The temperature sensitivity of (Ca, Sr)10Li(PO4)7:Ce3+, Mn2+ (CSLP:Ce3+, Mn2+) phosphor was explored by Zhang et al. [103] The temperature sensing potential of (Ca, Sr)10Li(PO4)7:Ce3+, Mn2+ phosphor was explored by FIR of the Ce3+ (violet) and Mn2+ (red) emission peaks. Ce3+ (violet) 's emission intensity gradually decreased while Mn2+ (red) increased with temperature. Fig. 6(c) demonstrates the temperature dependence of FIR of the Ce3+ (violet) and Mn2+ (red) emission peaks with temperature. Fig. 6(d) shows linear decrease in FIR with temperature. The inset of Fig. 6(d) reveals the temperature sensitivity of (Ca, Sr)10Li(PO4)7:Ce3+, Mn2+ (CSLP:Ce3+,Mn2+) phosphor. In another study, temperature sensing of rare earth ions doped Ca2Gd8(SiO4)6O2 upconversion phosphor was explored by Zhang. [104] This study evaluated the optical sensing properties of various lanthanide ions such as Er3+, Ho3+, and Tm3+ in Ca2Gd8(SiO4)6O2 host lattice. The best sensitivity achieved were 3.71 × 10−3 and 1.12 K − 1 for CGS:10%Yb3+, 3%Er3+, 0.111 K − 1 for CGS:10%Yb3+, 3%Ho3+ and 3.3 × 10−4 K − 1 for CGS:10%Yb3+,0.5%Tm3+. Although the Tm3+ doped phosphor has low sensitivity, it demi stated the high relative sensitivity. Recently, Wang and co-workers reported the high-efficiency NIR luminescent thermometry based on the thermal coupling of levels of Yb3+-Tm3+ doped Bi7F11O5 nanosheets [105]. In this study, it was observed that NIR luminescent thermometry of Bi7F11O5 nanosheets and doping Tm3+ activators into a polarized layered host. The Yb3+ and Tm3+ doped Bi7F11O5 nanosheets show a NIR to visible UC emission ratio (I800nm/I475nm) to 2000. The temperature dependent emission spectrum of Yb3+ and Tm3+ doped Bi7F11O5 temperature range from 303 K to 573 K is illustrated in Fig. 6(e). The intensity of emission bands at 475 nm and 800 nm decreases with temperature due to quenching effect while intensity of emission band at 700 nm increases slightly (Fig. 6(f)). These types of NIR to NIR optical sensors may serve as luminescent thermometer in biology with high penetration depth. Li et al. introduced the strategy to monitor the effect of temperature on upconversion emission of Ho3+ (or Tm3+)activated NaYF in real time by naked eye [106]. The Ho3+ (or Tm3+) activated NaYF phosphor shows change in blue, green and red upconversion emissions with temperature which can be recognizable by naked eye. It demonstrated that using this contactless thermometry technique, the chip temperature (Fig. 6(g)) can be monitor in reals time with naked eye (emission color changes from green to red, Fig. 6(h)). The red emission will increase with increase in local temperature of chip during operation which may help to prevent overheating failure of the chip. Various parameters such as emission intensity, spectrum position, FIR, lifetime etc. have been explored for luminescence thermometry and each of them has its own advantages and disadvantages. In most cases only one parameter is monitored for thermometry. Therefore, multimode thermometry becomes more significant which broaden and enhanced the working range where more than one parameter is monitored for thermometry which gives best performance. Kolesnikov et al. presented the multimode thermometry in YVO4:Er3+ phosphor in the temperature range of 299–466 K where luminescence intensity ratio, spectral line position and bandwidth were utilized for sensing temperature [32]. The very high relative thermal sensitivity of 2.61 0.61% K − 1 was achieved at room temperature for luminescence intensity ratio while ∼0.35% K − 1 for bandwidth and spectral position. Chi et al. reported Mn2+ doped Zn2GeO4 (ZGO) phosphor for multimode luminescence thermometry [107]. In this article, the author investigated temperature depends on luminescence lifetime and emission intensity in the temperature range of 250 to 420 K. The relative thermometry sensitivity for emission intensity and decay time for temperature sensing were 4.5% K − 1 and 4.6% K − 1, respectively. Michele Back et al. investigated lanthanide doped Bi2O3 phosphor for multimodal luminescence thermometry [108]. In multimodal luminescence thermometry; luminescence emission form Nd3+ design NIR-to-NIR Boltzmann based luminescent thermometers and the temperature dependence of the band gap edge tunning of host lattice are used to design reliable thermometric parameters. The highly crystalline Pr3+/Er3+ doped Bi2Mo2O9 phosphor for multimode thermometry was reported by Zhu et al. [109] Multimode luminescence thermometry utilized both down-conversion and upconversion fluorescence, along with their corresponding decay times. Temperature sensing using the downs-conversion emission bands of Pr3+/Er3+ co-doped Bi2Mo2O9 at 530 and 624 nm achieves absolute and relative sensitivities of 0.693 K − 1 at 568 K and 1.88% K − 1 at 483 K, respectively. Similarly, therefore, several articles are published on multimode luminescence thermometry [110–115].. There are several reports with various lanthanide doped ions in several host lattice such as Bi4Ti3O12, LiLaP4O12, Gd2ZnTiO6, NaBiF4, Bi7F11O5, KBaY(MoO4)3, NaYF4, A2LaNbO6 (A = Ba, Ca), LaF3 etc. which shows the potential of these phosphor for luminescent thermometry [116–125].

The spherical nanoparticles with physical dimensions equal or smaller than the exciton Bohr radius of the corresponding bulk materials are defined as quantum dots (QDs). Because of number of excellent optical properties such as higher emission intensity, narrow emission, high PL quantum yield, broad spectral windows spanning from the ultraviolet to infrared region quantum dots have explored for several application [28]. The application of quantum dots (QDs) for luminescence thermometry has gained huge interest from researchers and reported widely in the last few years [128–132]. In this section, we will discuss about the recent development in luminescent thermometry based on QDs. The use of luminescent CdTe quantum dots for temperature was demonstrated by Liang and co-workers [126]. In this study, they fabricated the ultrathin films by layer-by-layer assembly of CdTe QDs and layered double hydroxide (LDH) monolayer nanosheets and demonstrated its applications for temperature sensing. The temperature response of ultrathin films is display in Fig. 7 (a). The graph shows the linear decrease in luminescent intensity with an increase in temperature. The ultrathin films also exhibit significant and reversible change in luminescent intensity with temperature (23–80 ⁰C) as shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b). In this study it was also observed that the position of emission shifted from 558 nm to 568 nm with rise in temperature (left inset of Fig. 7(a)). Wu et al. reported the change in emission spectrum position with temperature [133]. The CH3NH3PbI3−
x Cl x films halides perovskite show the temperature dependence of fluorescence emission in the range of 77 K to 380 K. Tang et al. reported the emission of CsPbBr3 QDs changes from blue to green with a decrease in temperature [134]. It was observed that the emissions spectrum of show blue shift from 515 nm to 430 nm when temperatures is power than 0 °C. Although a slight red shift was also observed when the temperature rise above 50 °C. Nikoli´c et al. reported the Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles temperature sensing [127]. The Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles show the two emission peaks at 438 nm and 613 nm, attributed to trap emission from TiO2 and emission from f-f transition of Eu3+. Further, the temperature depends on the ratio of FIR between emission from Eu3+ ion and from TiO2 trap and decay time of emission from Eu3+ ion was determined. Both show the strong depends on temperature as show on Fig. 7(c). The temperature resolution is found to be better than 1 K as reported. However, temperature resolution observed 0.46 K for the fluorescence intensity ratio between emission from Eu3+ ion and TiO2 trap and 0.33 K for time decay measurements at ∼ 400 K. In another study, Tian et al. investigated the effect of size of particle on the upconversion (UC) luminescence and optical temperature sensitivity of Gd2O3:Yb3+/Er3+NPs [17]. Fig. 7(d) and (e) show the change in FIR of green emission of Gd2O3:Yb3+/Er3+ NPs with inverse absolute temperature and a function of temperature. It was observed that the thermometry sensitivity of Gd2O3:Yb3+/Er3+ NPs increases dramatically with a decrease in size, which may be owing to a more disordered local asymmetry environment in the smaller particles (owing to an increase in surface area with a decrease in size). The estimated thermometry sensitivity for Gd2O3:Yb3+/Er3+ nanoparticles with 45 nm was 0.0084 K − 1 at 570 upon 980 nm excitation. Pereira et al. demonstrated Yb3+/Tm3+ co-doped NaNbO3 NPs as a promising for high-resolution luminescent nanothermometers under three-photon-excited 940–975 nm laser [135]. The shape of the emission spectrum of Thulium (1G4→3H6) under excitation wavelength 908 nm was examined in the biophysical temperature. The Yb3+/Tm3+ co-doped NaNbO3 NPs exhibit two emission bands centered at 480 nm and 485 nm. The FIR between the emission bands at 480 nm and 485 nm was changed with a change in pumping intensity. The change in FIR of 480 nm and 485 nm emission band with pump power/intensity and induced local temperature is illustrated in Fig. 8 (a) and (b). The metal nanocluster could also be a promising candidate for physiological temperature due to facile synthesis approaches, exceptional photophysical properties, low toxicity, and better chemical stability. Kong et al. reported the gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) for thermometry sensing in the range of physiological temperature (0 to 60 °C range) [136]. It had been demonstrated that the fluorescent intensity of red emission of AuNCS depended on temperature. The dependence of fluorescent intensity of AuNCs with temperature is shown in Fig. 8(c). The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs decreases with temperature and decreases by 84% when the temperature rises from 279 to 313 K. Therefore, these AuNCs would be strong candidates for in human cells and in vivo temperature sensing. Lei et al.;explored the optical sensing behavior of lanthanide (Ln3+) doped Bi2O3 nanospheres [137]. The influence of temperature on FIR (I799/I808) of upconversion emission of Bi2O3:Yb3+/Tm3+ nanospheres is shown in Fig. 8(d). The Figure reveals that the FIR (I799/I808) of upconversion emission of Bi2O3:Yb3+/Tm3+ nanospheres increases with temperature. Carbon has been emerging as a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterials for various applications. Liu et al. investigated the optical temperature sensing of fluorescent ultra-thin films (UTFs) fabricated by layer-by-layer assembly of Carbon Dots (CDs) and 2D layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [138]. The variation in emission spectrum and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of UTFs (CDs@PSS/LDH) n in the temperature range 0 to 80 °C is demonstrated in Figs. 8(e) and (f), respectively. Figs. 8(e) and (f). exhibits that both the fluorescent and ECL of UTFs decreased with the increase in temperature. Therefore, UTFs (CDs@PSS/LDH) n could be a promising applicant for temperature sensing at 0 to 80 °C.

The thermometry sensing features of fluorescent bimetallic Au/Ag nanoclusters (NCs) in the environment temperature from 20 to 65 °C were studied by Huang et al. [139] Fig. 9 (a) shows the fluorescent emission spectrum of Au/Ag nanoclusters in the temperature range of 20 to 65 °C. The emission intensity of Au/Ag nanoclusters decreases with temperature. The decrease in emission intensity is because of the enhancement of the molecule collision frequency and non-radiative transition rate with temperature [140,141]. Moreover, the repeatability of sensing properties of Au/Ag was tested for up to eight consecutive cycles without any fatigue in the temperature range. In another study, the fluorescence-based temperature sensing of silica nanoparticle dual-response radiometric fluorescent sensor (DRFS) based was investigated by Chen and co-workers [142]. The FIR of two emission peaks at 576 and 445 nm was explored for the temperature sensing. The emission spectra of silica nanoparticles based DRFS is shown in Fig. 9(b). The Figure shows decrease in emission intensity of both emission peaks with temperature. The emission at 576 nm gradually and regularly decreases in intensity while 445 nm remain almost without change. Therefore, FIR of silica nanoparticle based DRFS shows linear change with temperature revealing the temperature sensing features. The thermometry sensing performance of Carbon (C)-dot was reported by Cui et al. [143] They investigated the influence of temperature on fluorescence emission of C-dots which shows a linear decrease with an increase in temperature in the range of 25–75⁰C, as illustrated in Fig. 9(c). It was also found that C-dots exhibit excellent reversibility and restorability when the temperature goes to 25⁰C. Therefore, the C-dot can be used for thermometry sensing with good reversibility, sensitivity, and linearity. The optical temperature sensing properties of ZnO:Yb/Tm nanoparticles was reported by Li et al. [144] It was observed that the blue and red emission of ZnO:Yb/Tm NPs were enhanced with rise in temperature. The enhancement emission may be due to the decrease in quenching centers with increase in annealing temperature. The change in the intensity of different emission peaks with increase in temperature is exhibited in Fig. 9(d) where G1, G2, G3 and G4 are assigned to 451, 475, 700 and 800 nm emission peaks. Fig. 9(d) reveals that the temperature sensing characteristic of ZnO:Yb/Tm nanoparticles.

Lewis et al. introduced the concept of monitoring temperature in microfluid devices using a machine learning model acquired from photoluminescence emission spectral and time-resolved PL of CdTe QDs as inputs [148]. The designed algorithm can measure the temperature in two temperature ranges: 10−300 K and 298−319 K. The achieved accuracy for the low-temperature regime and above 100 K was 7.7 K, or 0.4 K 0.1 K, respectively. The strategy for using self-assembly for measuring temperature was demonstrated by Jia et al. [145] Jia et al. prepared CD/AuNCs fluorescent hybrid nanomaterials via electrostatic interactions based on opposite surface charges of CDs and AuNCs. The PL emission spectra of CD/AuNCs fluorescent hybrid nanomaterials show a great response to temperature (Fig. 9(e) and (f)). The red emission from CD/AuNCs fluorescent hybrid nanomaterials upon 380 excitation varies with temperature from 20(C to 80(C) and shows reversible behavior as temperature is reduced high to low. In another study, the use of mercapto silane-passivated CuInS2 QDs for temperature measurement was reported by Marin and co-workers [146]. The luminescent thermometry of polymeric film embedding MPTS-passivated CuInS2 QDs was demonstrated in a broad temperature range from 140 to 340 K at excitation wavelength of 405 nm. The emission spectrum of polymer film exhibits the red shift with temperature increase as shown in Fig. 9(g). The thermal sensitivity obtained was above 1% K − 1 for temperatures greater than 280 K, which makes it a potential candidate for in-vitro luminescence thermometry. However, the luminescent thermometry in second and third biological optical windows (1000–1350 nm and 1550–1870) has gained potential researcher interest especially to biomedical treatment and diagnosis. Matuszewska et al. synthesized the SrTiO3:Ni2+, Er3+ nanocrystals and showed their use for luminescent thermometry in the second biological optical windows. The temperature depended on PL emission of SrTiO3:Ni2+, Er3+ nanocrystals in a wide temperature range of 150 and 210 °C,represented in Fig. 9(h). The PL emission intensity SrTiO3:Ni2+, Er3+ nanocrystal decreases with an increase in temperature, revealing its potential for luminescent thermometry [147]. There are a variety of nanomaterials such as Boron nitride (BN) QDs, Nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles, CDs, N, S co-doped carbon dots, LaPO4:Nd3+, YVO4:Eu3+, Tb3+/Pr3+:NaGd(MoO4)2, Mn-doped CsPb(Cl/Br)3, tin-halide perovskites, those have been shown temperature depended optical properties and presented a luminescent thermometer [149–159].

The core@shell materials have gained great attention owing to their exceptional chemical and physical properties and potential in various applications like optoelectronics, anti-counterfeiting, photovoltaic, sensing etc. [160,161]. The core-shell materials are a special type of structure which consist of a core (inner central) particle and a shell (outer-layer) on core [162]. Compared to other multicomponent nanomaterials, the core@shell materials have been widely investigated owing to their outstanding properties, which can be easily tailored to specific application by altering core material, core size, shell materials, shell thickness [162]. In last few decades, core@shell materials also explored extensively for temperature thermometer because of excellent optical properties [163–166]. Xu et al. reported a novel flower-like α-NaYb(Mn)F4:Er3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 upconversion NPs based on variation in intensity ratios between emission bands of Er3+ (2H11/2 → 4I15/2 and 4S3/2 → 4I15/2) and Tm3+ (1G4 → 3H5 and 3H4 → 3H6) with temperature under 980 nm excitation wavelength [1]. The change in shape of the emission spectrum of Er3+ and Tm3+ and FIR between bands decreased with an increase in temperature in the range of 123−423 K. The variation in the upconversion emission of α-NaYb(Mn)F4:Er3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 NPs might be due to change in the phonon density with temperature. Xu et al. claims that the results were in agreement with Boltzmann-type population distribution[1]. The upconversion FIR of Er3+ (REr; I522/I540) and Tm3+ (RTm; I776/I803) as a function of temperature is demonstrated in Figs. 10 (a) and (b), respectively. Moreover, the thermometry sensitivity of Er3+ and Tm3+-doped α-NaYb(Mn)F4@NaYF4 differed. The thermometry sensitivity of α-NaYb(Mn)F4:Er3+@NaYF4 increases with temperature while decreases with an increase in temperature for α-NaYb(Mn)F4:Tm3+@NaYF4 nanoparticles. The maximum sensitivities were 0.0049 K − 1 at 423 K and 0.0024 K − 1 at 123 K for α-NaYb(Mn)F4:Er3+@NaYF4 and α-NaYb(Mn)F4:Tm3+@NaYF4 NPs, respectively as shown in Fig. 10(c) and (d), respectively. The effect of silica shell on temperature sensing properties of Er, Yb:GdVO4 upconversion nanoparticles was reported by Savchuk et al. [2] Moreover, it was observed that silica coated upconversion nanoparticles had more sustainability for longer under 980 nm excitation compared to nanoparticles without coating. The dependence of FIR of emission spectrum and thermometry sensitivity of Er, Yb: GdVO4 and Er,Yb:GdVO4@SiO2 NPs on temperature is show in Fig. 10(e) and (f). The estimated temperature sensitivity for core-shell Er,Yb:GdVO4@SiO2 nanoparticles (1.01% K − 1) was double as compared to bare Er,Yb: GdVO4 nanoparticles (0.69% K − 1).

Liu et al. presented the dual mode luminescent thermometry with PbS/CdS/CdSe core/barrier/shell quantum dots (QDs) [167]. The PbS/CdS/CdSe quantum dots (QDs) exhibits two emission peaks located at 670 and 910 nm, which show temperature-dependence in the 180−300 K range with thermal sensitivity of 1.22% K − 1. The PbS/CdS/CdSe QDs dispersed in poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) and the thin film was deposited on the glass substrate in order to explore luminescent thermometry application (Fig. 11 (a)). The brightness of images was increased with a decrease in temperature (Fig. 11(a)). The temperature-dependent emission spectra of PbS/CdS/CdSe QDs at excitation 560 nm in temperature range 100 to 300 K is illustrated in Fig. 11(b). The intensities of both emission bands at 670 and 910 nm increase with a decrease in temperature due to a decline in trapping by defects/traps and assisted thermal escape. In another study, Shang et al. introduced the dual mode upconversion luminescent thermometry using ultrasmall α-NaYbF4:Tm3+@CaF2@NaYF4:Yb3+/Er3+@CaF2 core/multishell NPs in a temperature range of 10–295 K [168]. The ultrasmall α-NaYbF4:Tm3+@CaF2@NaYF4:Yb3+/Er3+@CaF2 core/multishell nanoparticles show room temperature (150–290 K) and ultralow (10 to 290 K) sensitivity attributed to Tm3+ and Er3+ ions, respectively (Fig. 11(c) and (d)). Fig. 11(c) that the emission band intensity at 521 nm (Er3+:2H11/2 → 4I15/2) is very low at low temperature while there is a significant increase when the temperature increases from 150 to 295 K. The α-NaYbF4:Tm3+ core shows temperature dependence even in low temperature (below 150 K) (Fig. 11(d)). Geitenbeek et al. reported NaYF4:Er3+, Yb3+/SiO2 NPs for luminescent thermometry upto 900 K [169]. The temperature deepened upconversion spectrum of NaYF4:Er3+, Yb3+ and NaYF4:Er3+, Yb3+/SiO2 nanocrystal is demonstrated in Fig. 11(e) and (f). Both emission spectra show an increase in upconversion emission with the increase in temperature. The NaYF4:Er3+, Yb3+ nanocrystal show temperature depends up to 600 K. At higher temperature the intensity dropped, making it too low to determine temperatures accurately. This may be due to the oxidation of the organic ligands capping the NCs. Consequently, SiO2 shell strongly increase the luminescent thermometry capability high up to the highest temperature (900 K). Many core-shell nanostructures such as GdOF@SiO2, YVO4:Er3+, Yb3+@Nd3+, CdSe/ZnS QDs, β‐NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+@SiO2, Bi2SiO5@SiO2, SiO2@Au@SiO2@QD, CsPbBr3/EuPO4, CuInS2/ZnS QDs, Ag/Ag2S core shell nanoparticles, LuVO4:Yb3+/Er3+@SiO2, and NaYF4:Er3+/Yb3+@NaYF4:Tm3+/Yb3+ reported for luminescent thermometry which reveals their potential for next generation optical sensor [170–180].

The discoveries and development of modern chemistry could be impossible without involvement organic compounds because organic compounds are easily amenable, cheaper and easy for device fabrication [181]. The luminescent organic compounds exhibit fluorescence which arises from the triplet to singlet state relaxations of the excited triplet-states (forbidden radiative) and have been explored in various application such as optoelectronic, bio-imaging, drug delivery, display and lighting devices, sensing etc. due to excellent optical properties. In recent times, different organic compounds have been extensively explored as thermometers for luminescent thermometry. This suggests that organic compounds with temperature-depended luminescent features could be promising for non-contact temperature measurement in the desired range of temperature. Miyata and co-workers developed luminescent lanthanide complexes dye to design thermosensing dye for the temperature sensor [182]. The synthesized chameleon luminophore [Tb0.99Eu0.01 (hfa)3(dpbp)]n shows high luminescent with a quantum yield of about 40% and is thermally stable. The luminophore [Tb0.99Eu0.01 (hfa)3(dpbp)]n shows luminescent-depended temperature sensing in a wide range of temperatures 200–500 K. The optical photograph of [Tb0.99Eu0.01 (hfa)3(dpbp)]n at different temperatures under 365 nm irradiation as illustrated in Fig. 12 (a). Li et al. developed the organic counterion-induced vesicles (OCIVs) based reversible temperature sensor [183]. The water soluble dye HPTS (8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonic acid) was introduced into organic counterion-induced vesicles (OCIVs).

The optical temperature sensitivity of the combination of HPTS-OCIVs was investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity in the range of 37–70⁰C. The variation in fluorescence intensity of HPTS-OCIVs with temperature is shown in Fig. 12(b). Fig. 12(b) reveals that the fluorescence intensity decreases with the temperature increase and exhibits reverse behavior when temperature decreases. The optical thermometry sensing features of Pt and Pd benzoporphyrin dyes were investigated by Zach et al. [184] The influence of temperature on emission spectra of Pd-O-S in PSAN under anoxic conditions and air saturation are shown in Fig. 12(c) and (d), respectively. The emission spectra show that the NIR phosphorescence gradually decreases while red emission regularly increases with temperature. The variation emission spectrum of Pd-O-S in PSAN under anoxic conditions and air saturation is almost similar. Moreover, Steinegger et al. explored the optical temperature sensing properties of organic thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emitters (carbazole-substituted dicyanobenzenes and anthraquinone based intramolecular charge-transfer dyes) [184]. These dyes show a decrease in thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) intensity with an increase in temperature. Further, the TADF lifetime also exhibits the temperature dependence. The −1.4 to −3.7% change in TADF lifetime was observed, with change per K showing excellent temperature sensitivity. Zhang et al. demonstrated gold nanocluster-embedded peptide nanofibers for temperature sensors [185]. The dependence of the intensity of emission spectrum of peptide nanofibers- gold nanoclusters (PNF-AuNCs) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution is illustrated in Fig. 13 (a). Further, Fig. 13(a) demonstrated the decrease in luminescence intensity with temperature 10 to 45 °C. The fluorescent intensity decreases by 52% at 45 °C. Sun and co-workers reported that near-infrared gold nanoclusters could be nanoprobe for temperature sensing [186]. In this report, the change in the near-infrared fluorescence of gold nanoclusters with temperature was studied. The emission spectra of GSH–AuNCs at different temperature heating (0 to 80 °C) and cooling (80 to 0 °C) are illustrated in Figs. 13(b) and (c). Figs. 12(d) show that the fluorescence intensity of GSH–AuNCs decreased with temperature and recovered to the initial value when cooling to the preliminary temperature.

Yang and co-workers fabricated the Tb/Eu-based mixed lanthanide metal-organic framework (LnMOF) films that show luminescent thermometry properties attributed to Tb's temperature-dependent emission intensity ratio/Eu [187]. This mixed lanthanide metal–organic framework (LnMOF) shows radiometric thermometry properties in the temperature range of 298–383 K with a high relative sensitivity of 16.14% K − 1 at 359 K. The emission intensity of two emission peaks at 549 nm (corresponding to Tb3+) and 617 nm (corresponding to Eu3+) display a steady decline and appreciably enhanced with temperature, respectively (Fig. 14 (a)). This leads to a change in emission color from green to red with an increase in temperature (Fig. 14(b)). The author also demonstrated that by LnMOF films contacting with flower patterned hot surface, the naked eyes can directly observe a spatial variation of emission in LnMOF films (Fig. 14(c) and (d)). Kaczmarek et al. designed a series of three bipyridines periodic mesoporous organosilica (BPy-PMOs) grafted with lanthanide β-diketonate complex, which show the capability to sense temperature [188]. In another study, Zhao and a co-worker reported the synthesis of lanthanide-doped mixed LnMOFs, Tb0.95Eu0.05cpna and Tb0.95Eu0.05bpydc that show potential ratiometric luminescent thermometry in a temperature range of 25–300 K [189]. The emission color of these lanthanide doped mixed LnMOFs, Tb0.95Eu0.05cpna and Tb0.95Eu0.05bpydc shifted from green to red with increase from 25 to 300 K (Fig. 14(e) and (f)). Tom et al. designed a stimuli-responsive coordination polymer, [ZnL2]n.nH2O (H2L = 2, 3-butanedionebisisonicotinylhydrazone) for luminescent thermometry [190]. The emission color of coordination polymer, [ZnL2]n.nH2O (H2L = 2, 3-butanedionebisisonicotinylhydrazone) changes from yellow to red with rise in temperature from 40 to 90 °C which can be recognized by naked eyes. Kitagawa et al. prepared the Eu(III) complexes and demonstrated their luminescent thermometry originating from ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) [191]. Kaczmarek and co-workers synthesized a mesoporous silica decorated with dipyridyl‐pyridazine (dppz) ligands and mixed with Eu3+/Tb3+, which show capability to measure temperature in the 10–360 K temperature range [192]. Recently, lanthanide based complexes are also explored for polarization of luminescence for thermometry. Bobrovsky et al. demonstrated the use of cholesteric copolymer doped with a europium complex for polarized luminescence-based thermometry [193]. The strong temperature dependence of the wavelength of selective light reflection provides a high thermos-sensitivity of the degree of circular polarization of the europium complex emission. In this study thermal sensitivity up to 65%/K of the planar aligned samples was achieved in this study. Sun et al. reported the temperature-dependent dual-mode fluorescence and switchable circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) in a new family of triarylborane-based π-systems, 2,2′-diamino-6,6′-diboryl-1,1′-binaphthyls which depends on the chosen amino groups [194]. There are numerous reports on various compounds such as cycTb-phEu and cycEu-phTb, Tb1–xEuxFTPTC (x = 0.05, 0.1, 0.2), Eu3+/Tb3+ lanthanide complexes and poly(methylmethacrylate), Eu3+/Tb3+-mixed MOFs ([(CH3)2NH2]Eu0.036Tb0.964BPTC), which investigated as a potential thermometer in various temperature ranges [195–200].

The hybrid nanomaterials consist of a mixture of two or more different components which are chemically different and formed due to various interaction such as hydrogen bonding, Van der Waals, covalent bonds, or weak electrostatic interactions [201,202]. In hybrid nanomaterials, inorganic compounds such as inorganic QDs, metal clusters or particles, oxides, sulfides, and their derivatives, etc. are integrated with organic components like ligands, organic groups or molecules, polymers, metal-organic-frameworks (MOFs) via specific interactions in order to enhancement of their functional properties for a variety of applications. The possibilities of hybrid nanomaterials have been studied for temperature sensing in various research articles [145,203-207]. Chen et al. demonstrated a hybrid luminescent upconversion nanomaterial synthesis with Eu(TTA)3(TPPO)2 for temperature sensing applications [208]. These hybrid nanoparticles have dual emission at 544 nm (upconversion) and 616 nm (down-conversion), dual mode temperature sensing. The dual mode temperature depends of the luminescence emission of hybrid nanomaterials is shown in Figs. 15 (a) and (b). This hybrid nanomaterial's dual temperature-dependent emission makes it a favorable contender for temperature sensing. In another study, the potential use of hybrid of upconversion nanoparticles with R6G is used for temperature sensor [209]. The change in upconversion luminescence intensity was proposed due to the energy transfer between upconversion nanoparticles and R6G. The hybrid upconversion nanoparticles exhibit better temperature sensitivity compared to pure upconversion nanocrystals, as illustrated in Fig. 15(c).

Moreover, the effect of Au NPs on optical temperature sensing properties of NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ upconversion nanophosphor was explored by Li et al. [210] The distance between the NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ and Au nanoparticles was adjusted by NaYF4 shell. Although, there was a slight decrease in the intensity of upconversion emission of NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ nanophosphor. However, this decrease was reduced to negligible by NaYF4 shell, which significantly improved the upconversion emission of NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ nanophosphor. Ramalho et al. reported the organic–inorganic hybrids based on europium (Eu3+) and terbium (Tb3+) ions, which display instantaneous response to temperature in the range of 12–318 K [211]. The luminescent QR codes designed with hybrid materials have dual mode capability, i.e. the storage capacity and temperature sensing together which can be monitor in real-time using a photo taken with the charge-coupled smartphone device. Salmon and a co-worker designed EuW10@UiO-67 hybrid by introducing EuW10O36]9− polyoxometalate into the cavities of the highly porous zirconium luminescent metal-organic framework UiO-67. The EuW10@UiO-67 hybrid shows a self-calibrated-ratiometric thermometer in the physiological range [212]. Wang et al. synthesized highly luminescent RhB@ZIF-365 from Rhodamine B (RhB) and zeolite imidazole framework ZIF-365, which can measure the temperature with thermometric sensitivity 0.54% °C − 1 and temperature resolution better than 0.1 °C [213]. Few other reports demonstrated hybrid materials could be potential candidates for temperature sensing [214–217].

Apart from the above examples, a few hybrid or bi-functional multimodal nanomaterials for effective in-vivo thermal therapies and bioimaging have shown the possibilities of monitoring the heat at the nanoscale through optical nanothermometry in visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions. In fact, for many in-vivo applications, the NIR region is interesting due to penetration depth issues (few millimeters). One interesting hybrid material could be magnetic and luminescence nanoentities into one entity, for example, via core-shell engineering. Such systems responses the heating behavior by AC magnetic field and laser actions. The luminescence unit may show the emission energies coupled with temperature rise. Earlier, Huang et al. prepared Mn-ferrite-based NPs coupled with dylight 549 dye (conjugated to the streptavidin-coated Mn-ferrite NPs on the cell membrane Golgi-targeted green fluorescent protein (GFP) (co-expressed within the cell)), and temperature dependent emission data was collected by green laser excitation [218]. The temperature sensitivity of such dyes is ∼1.5%. Similarly, Piñol et al. designed a ferrofluid of maghemite multicore nanostructure coated with the P4VP-Eu3+/Tb3+ polymer complex ions. They induced the heat using AC magnetic field (magnetic hyperthermia) and simultaneously irradiated a UV light (365 nm) to visualize the dual emission patterns of the Eu3+/Tb3+ complexes. The analysis of the obtained results showed the highest thermal sensitivity (5.8% K − 1 at 296 K) and the low thermometer heat capacitance (0.021·K–1) and thermometer resistance (1 K·W–1) [218]. Tejada et al. prepared YAG:Nd/Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell nanocomposites by mixing superparamagnetic iron-oxide and luminescent Y2Al15O12:Nd3+ and shell of SiO2 which was heated to 57 °C when exposed to AC magnetic field with particular frequency and amplitude [219]. Simultaneously, it was excited at 813 nm, and calculations displayed a thermal sensitivity of 0.14% K − 1. Recently, a very interesting work performed by Ortgies et al., proposes mixed magnetite NPs and LaF3:Nd encapsulation in the poly-lactic-co-glycolic acid polymer for multimodal actions such as magnetic hyperthermia, photothermal therapies, and optical nanothermometry in NIR window (excitation wavelength 808 nm) [220]. A multiparametric approach (wavelength shifting and intensity variations) agreed with the thermal sensitivity of 0.4% K − 1 while observing thermal couple two peaks (862.2 and 864 nm) due to thermally induced strains in the Nd3+ environment leading to electron−phonon interaction. Additionally, the research group of Bakuzis reported a multimodal combined magnetic and photo hyperthermia, and attempted to under the luminescent nanothermomtery properties in a core-shell-shell system [221]. A low optimized field magnetic hyperthermic Zn0.3Mn0.7Fe2O4/cit. was coated with Nd3+-embedded SiO2 helped to understand the basic mechanism of enhanced photo hyperthermia along with luminescence naothermometry. But a few deleterious effects of the photothermal conversion efficiency and nanothermomeric luminescence signal was reduced by additiing another thin layer of SiO2, i.e. Zn0.3Mn0.7Fe2O4@SiO2@SiO2:Nd3+ NPs. Through this, photothermal conversion efficiency increases to 18%. As a potential nanothermometer, with excitation around 800 nm and emission at the second biological window, the thermal sensitivity of the system was found to be ∼1.1% K–1 at 300 K (27 °C), ∼1.4% K–1at 316 K (43 °C), and ∼1.5% K–1 at 319 K (46 °C). Such nano-systems are quite interesting in many biological applications where the temperature at the cellular level is required [206,222-224].

In summary, we reviewed various luminescent materials and proposed for future luminescent thermometry to achieve non-contact thermal sensing with high resolution. Luminescent thermometry is an accurate, reliable, high resolution, and precise method for the measurement of surface temperature. In this review, we discussed about the several luminescent materials; lanthanide-based materials, nanoparticles and quantum dots, core@shell systems, organic compounds, and hybrid nanomaterials for thermometry, and how these luminescent materials have been used as thermometry probes, in their temperature range, excitation wavelength, sensitivity and resolution. Most of the luminescent materials show temperature depended on luminescence properties. Depending upon the actual system to be thermally probed, accordingly most suitable material can be used. Consequently, luminescent materials provided endless number of opportunities to design luminescence-based temperature sensors.

Further, numerous methods and luminescent materials have been used to readout the temperature by luminescence thermometry which can be adopted for someone's specific problem. The choice of luminescent materials is critical to achieve high sensitivity and accuracy for particular application. The choice of materials depends upon number of factors such as temperature range of interest, acceptable resolution, cost of materials, measurement environment and availability of materials [225]. Different materials used for luminescence thermometry have their advantages and disadvantages. For example, the lanthanide doped luminescent materials have an advantage over other luminescent materials like QDs, Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), and organic dye due to their high thermometry sensitivity at higher temperatures. On the other side low thermal and chemical stability as well as photobleaching of QDs, Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), organic dye limits their potential in thermometry. The toxicity of a few luminescent QDs also limits their thermometry specially in bio-medical applications. In some case luminescent organic ligands may have an advantage over lanthanide doped metamaterials their higher absorptions as organic ligands also act as antennas, and because of this excitation wavelengths are shifted into the visible spectral region which significantly reduces autofluorescence in case of tissue thermometry.

One of the major advantages of luminescence thermometry is that it offers contactless or semi-non-contact temperature sensing (which means no direct thermometer and reader coupling) with high sensitivity. Besides the choice of materials used for luminescent thermometry, the method adopted for thermometry measurement also has its appropriate scope with their its own advantages and limitations. For example, when a change in intensity of emission spectrum is used for temperature sensing it looks very attractive. But intensity of emission spectrum depends on many factors such as distance between temperature probe and detector, type of medium/environment, local concentration of phosphor/doping, and intensity power of excitation source. Due to the dependence on multiple factors, precise and accurate temperature measurement becomes complicated. However, when a change in single band ratio or shape/bandwidth of the emission spectrum is used for temperature sensing then it could be more reliable because it didn't depend on the concentration of luminescence material or dopant, distance between probe and detector and other experimental parameters. The sensitivity of thermometry could be effective by medium/environment because scattering and dispersion of excitation source depends on medium which leads to changes in intensity of excitation which may has effect on emission spectrum resulting in influence on accuracy of temperature measurements [226]. Although the above discussed methods are simple and direct for thermometry but is some case thermal quenching may also affect the measurement. Compared to other parameters such as intensity, shape, position, and width of emission spectrum, the lifetime or decay time has many advantages in thermometry because it is not affected by external conditions, instrument is more accurate in time (τ) resolution than in spectral resolution. Additionally, the impact of temperature on intensity, shape, position, width of emission spectrum is more as compare decay time or lifetime at higher temperatures. Therefore, the thermal quenching is low, and the signal-to-noise ratio is also minimum which makes this method more accurate. However, this method is not economical because cost of setup for lifetime measurement is expensive due to requirements of fast response electronic and pulsed excitation light source [227].

For the development of futuristic luminescence thermometry sensor or probes, there are two critical requirements: (i) sensor should have higher PL quantum yield and (ii) offer higher signal changes with the variation over a specific temperature range. These increase the sensitivity of luminescent sensor. For the highly sensitive temperature sensor, the sensitivity should be in the order of ∼0.01 K. Another important concern about luminescent based thermal sensing is the thermal equilibrium of luminescent materials and surface. This will depend upon various factor such as coating/ luminescent materials used, radiative heat transfer, and the temperature levels. Further, it also needs to develop a spectral analysis which can estimates multiple lifetime/amplitude spectra because there are only limited luminescent materials with single- exponential luminescence decay. Only, reports mentioned about the reversible luminescence feature luminescent materials under variation in temperature. Therefore, the systematic studies of reversible nature of luminescence-based thermometer should be examined in detail. Furthermore, the luminescent materials used for sensor should have high thermal stability. There should not be any change in crystal structure of luminescent materials after the exposer heat. The luminescent feature of materials should remain appropriately stable for whole operational lifetime. For futuristic luminescence thermometry sensors, the Stock shift between the excitation and emission should be longer. This will reduce the interferences caused by the emission color of the luminescent material and excitation source particularly under strong UV or shortwave visible excitation wavelengths. The sensor should be thermally stable, cover wider ranges with adequate resolution, capability to measure high temperature, and not to be toxic or harmful. However, the development of advanced thermometry sensor which can operate within the biological window (700–900 nm) is the need of the hour. Conclusively, no need for a universal thermometry sensor. The thermometry sensor can be developed with various techniques depending upon the specific needs. The above discussed points will be challenging in the future for designing upcoming luminescent thermometry sensors. Finally, this review provides an outlook about methods and luminescent materials used for thermometry sensors. In the past few, great efforts have been devoted to developing luminescent materials for temperature sensors. Future years will undoubtedly see significant and exciting breakthroughs because of the cross-fertilization of thoughts and experiences at these interfaces.
